channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: NASA Reboot Memo From Jared Isaacman (long) - NASA Watch
item.description:
1. NASA Reboot Memo From Jared Isaacman (long)  NASA Watch
  2. NASA Announces Realignment to Accelerate Mission Delivery  NASA (.gov)
  3. NASA unveils sweeping reorganization  SpaceNews
  4. NASA is updating its Artemis moon base plan. You can find out how on May 26.  Space
  5. To achieve major goals, NASA seeks to streamline its organization  Ars Technica

item.title: A fossil site in North Dakota appears to have captured the day the dinosaur-killing asteroid struck Earth, right down to tiny glass beads from the impact lodged in the gills of fish that died within hours - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A fossil site in North Dakota appears to have captured the day the dinosaur-killing asteroid struck Earth, right down to tiny glass beads from the impact lodged in the gills of fish that died within hours  Space Daily
  2. How did animals survive the asteroid that killed the dinosaurs?  Live Science
  3. The Chicxulub impact signature in East Asia - Communications Earth & Environment  Nature
  4. Japanese researchers discover Chicxulub impact evidence tied to dinosaur extinction  The Watchers - Watching the world evolve and transform
  5. New evidence has been found in Japan of an asteroid that may have wiped out the dinosaurs  UA.NEWS

item.title: The Milky Way ate another galaxy. Scientists say they’ve found the scraps - CNN
item.description: The Milky Way ate another galaxy. Scientists say they’ve found the scraps  CNNSee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: Caltech could lose control of JPL for the first time - Los Angeles Times
item.description:
1. Caltech could lose control of JPL for the first time  Los Angeles Times
  2. NASA to Compete Contract for Jet Propulsion Laboratory Management  NASA (.gov)
  3. After 68 Years, Caltech Will Have to Bid to Keep JPL Contract  Pasadena Now
  4. NASA Is Opening Up Bids For Who Will Run The Jet Propulsion Laboratory  Engadget
  5. Shakeup at JPL? Control over iconic NASA center could change for 1st time in nearly 100 years  Space

item.title: Fossil Kept in a Museum For Decades Turns Out to Be a Fearsome New Predator - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Fossil Kept in a Museum For Decades Turns Out to Be a Fearsome New Predator  ScienceAlert
  2. Paleontologists Discover an Ancient Marine Reptile They've Dubbed the T. Rex of the Sea, Crowning Another King of the Cretaceous  Smithsonian Magazine
  3. ‘T. rex of the sea’: D-FW researchers identify new ancient marine species  Dallas News
  4. Ancient seas get a new T. rex as massive mosasaur emerges from Texas fossils  Phys.org
  5. Tylosaurus Rex: Scientists name formidable extinct 43-foot ocean predator from Ice Age  Yahoo

item.title: Research Paper Warns That There's a Massive Experiment at Work to Geoengineer the Earth's Climate - Futurism
item.description:
1. Research Paper Warns That There's a Massive Experiment at Work to Geoengineer the Earth's Climate  Futurism
  2. Research Paper Warns That There’s a Massive Experiment at Work to Geoengineer the Earth’s Climate  Yahoo

item.title: A NASA satellite launched in 1976 carries a Carl Sagan–designed plaque sealed inside its core, mapping Earth's continents 268 million years ago, at launch, and 8.4 million years from now — and that last date is no accident, because it's roughly when the sat - Space Daily
item.description: A NASA satellite launched in 1976 carries a Carl Sagan–designed plaque sealed inside its core, mapping Earth's continents 268 million years ago, at launch, and 8.4 million years from now — and that last date is no accident, because it's roughly when the sat  Space Daily

item.title: Webb just clocked nearly 9,000 young star clusters and found the biggest ones break from their birth clouds in 5 million years, a timing clue that could reshape how astronomers model galaxies growing up - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Webb just clocked nearly 9,000 young star clusters and found the biggest ones break from their birth clouds in 5 million years, a timing clue that could reshape how astronomers model galaxies growing up  Space Daily
  2. Webb Studies Star Clusters  NASA (.gov)
  3. Webb Reveals M51’s Rapid Star Cluster Emergence in the Whirlpool Galaxy  TechEBlog -
  4. CNS Astronomer Daniela Calzetti Looks into the Cradle Where Stars are Born : College of Natural Sciences  UMass Amherst
  5. Fall into the Whirlpool Galaxy  Astronomy Magazine

item.title: The Voyager Golden Record carries a small sample of uranium on its cover, placed there so that whoever finds it can measure the decay and work out how long it has been drifting — a built-in clock for a message engineered to last around a billion years. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Voyager Golden Record carries a small sample of uranium on its cover, placed there so that whoever finds it can measure the decay and work out how long it has been drifting — a built-in clock for a message engineered to last around a billion years.  Space Daily
  2. The Voyager Golden Record carries greetings in 55 languages — a deliberate attempt to send a small sample of human voices into deep space long after the spacecraft fell silent.  Space Daily
  3. The Voyager probes carry golden records etched with greetings in 55 languages, whale songs, and a woman's brainwaves recorded while she thought about being in love, and the records are designed to remain playable for one billion years drifting through i  Space Daily

item.title: Titan’s atmosphere is thicker than Earth’s, its rivers and lakes are made of methane and ethane, and NASA is sending a nuclear-powered drone there because on Saturn’s largest moon, flying may be easier than driving. - Space Daily
item.description: Titan’s atmosphere is thicker than Earth’s, its rivers and lakes are made of methane and ethane, and NASA is sending a nuclear-powered drone there because on Saturn’s largest moon, flying may be easier than driving.  Space Daily

item.title: Stephen Hawking’s dad reveals super genius was lazy kid who ‘didn’t study,’ lounged around too much: biography - New York Post
item.description: Stephen Hawking’s dad reveals super genius was lazy kid who ‘didn’t study,’ lounged around too much: biography  New York PostSee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: Neptune’s Moon Nereid May Be Lone Survivor of an Ancient Cosmic Massacre - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Neptune’s Moon Nereid May Be Lone Survivor of an Ancient Cosmic Massacre  Gizmodo
  2. Astronomers believe Neptunian moon is lone intact survivor of ancient collision  CNN
  3. One of Neptune's 16 moons is not like the others, James Webb telescope finds — and it could be key to fully understanding the solar system  Live Science
  4. Neptune’s Moon Nereid Might Be the Sole Intact Survivor of an Ancient Lunar Collision  Smithsonian Magazine
  5. This Moon of Neptune Might Have Survived a Wrecking-Ball Event  The New York Times

item.title: A slice of the static on an old untuned television was the afterglow of the Big Bang, which means millions of people spent decades staring at the oldest light in the universe without knowing it. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A slice of the static on an old untuned television was the afterglow of the Big Bang, which means millions of people spent decades staring at the oldest light in the universe without knowing it.  Space Daily
  2. Space wasn’t infinitely small when the hot Big Bang began  Big Think
  3. Cosmically Curious: Before the Big Bang  New Hampshire Public Radio

item.title: The Vela satellites were built to catch secret nuclear tests, but they accidentally recorded flashes from deep space that opened a new branch of astrophysics - Space Daily
item.description: The Vela satellites were built to catch secret nuclear tests, but they accidentally recorded flashes from deep space that opened a new branch of astrophysics  Space Daily

item.title: Sea level rise is speeding up and scientists now know exactly why - ScienceDaily
item.description:
1. Sea level rise is speeding up and scientists now know exactly why  ScienceDaily
  2. Less low cloud cover lets in more heat from the sun—and may lock in centuries of sea level rise  Phys.org
  3. Climate: Seas rise mainly due to expansion of warmer water | blue News  blue News
  4. Oceans are heating faster than we think  MSN
  5. Chinese study shows sea levels are rising twice as fast as they were in 1960  Earth.com

item.title: Atomic oxygen in low Earth orbit slowly eats spacecraft surfaces, and the ISS survives because engineers learned to coat, test, and replace the materials most vulnerable to it - Space Daily
item.description: Atomic oxygen in low Earth orbit slowly eats spacecraft surfaces, and the ISS survives because engineers learned to coat, test, and replace the materials most vulnerable to it  Space Daily

item.title: The Hubble Deep Field began as a gamble on a tiny patch of sky that had been chosen because it looked almost empty, and it ended by revealing nearly 3,000 galaxies hiding in what seemed like nothing. - Space Daily
item.description: The Hubble Deep Field began as a gamble on a tiny patch of sky that had been chosen because it looked almost empty, and it ended by revealing nearly 3,000 galaxies hiding in what seemed like nothing.  Space Daily

item.title: Mysterious Earthquakes Strike Like Clockwork – We May Finally Know Why - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Mysterious Earthquakes Strike Like Clockwork – We May Finally Know Why  ScienceAlert
  2. After 30 years, Scientists Solve Mystery Of Deep Pacific Earthquake That Repeats Like Clockwork  IFLScience
  3. ‘The tick-tick quake’: Scientists crack the code of the world’s most frequent earthquakes in pacific  The Times of India
  4. Scientists solve the 30-year mystery of ‘clockwork’ earthquakes  The Brighter Side of News
  5. Scripps Geophysicist Helps Solve Mystery of Earthquake System Deep beneath Pacific  Scripps Institution of Oceanography |

item.title: Earth's outer core beneath Pacific reversed direction in 2010, satellite data reveal - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Earth's outer core beneath Pacific reversed direction in 2010, satellite data reveal  Phys.org
  2. Earth’s molten outer core is behaving in chaotic, unexpected ways  Scientific American
  3. Earth’s molten core flow 1999 and 2016  European Space Agency
  4. New analysis sheds light on unexpected reversal in Earth’s molten outer core beneath the Pacific  The Watchers - Watching the world evolve and transform

item.title: Scientists Found The Atomic Reason That Gold Refuses to Rust - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Scientists Found The Atomic Reason That Gold Refuses to Rust  ScienceAlert
  2. How does gold keep its glitter? Researchers uncover why it resists tarnish  Phys.org
  3. Physics explains why gold stays pristine  Science News
  4. Scientists Identify Atomic Trick That Keeps Gold Shiny  Gizmodo
  5. Gold Atoms Rearrange To Block Oxidation In Seconds  Quantum Zeitgeist

item.title: Scientists thought Jupiter's moon Europa was ejecting water. Now they're not so sure - Yahoo
item.description:
1. Scientists thought Jupiter's moon Europa was ejecting water. Now they're not so sure  Yahoo
  2. It Looks Like Europa Doesn't Have Plumes of Water Vapour After All  Universe Today
  3. So Those Water Vapors on Europa Might Not Actually Be a Thing  Gizmodo
  4. Findings reconsider the existence of Europa's vapor plumes  Phys.org
  5. The case for water plumes on Europa just got much harder to make  AOL.com

item.title: A glacier bigger than Washington state is melting faster than ever. The implications are dire - OregonLive.com
item.description:
1. A glacier bigger than Washington state is melting faster than ever. The implications are dire  OregonLive.com
  2. The ‘doomsday’ glacier’s giant ice shelf is about to break away  New Scientist
  3. Map Shows US Cities That Could Go Underwater if Sea Levels Rise 3 Meters  Newsweek
  4. Global Warming Icon “Doomsday Glacier” in Trouble  CounterPunch.org
  5. Doomsday Glacier Shows Signs of Imminent Disintegration  Futurism

item.title: I spent years assuming my personality was fixed — then I learned what neuroplasticity actually means and realised I had been maintaining myself like a finished product instead of a living system - Space Daily
item.description: I spent years assuming my personality was fixed — then I learned what neuroplasticity actually means and realised I had been maintaining myself like a finished product instead of a living system  Space Daily

item.title: Scientists Finally Think They Know Why T. rex Had Tiny Arms - SciTechDaily
item.description:
1. Scientists Finally Think They Know Why T. rex Had Tiny Arms  SciTechDaily
  2. Scientists May Finally Know Why T. Rex Had Such Tiny Arms  ScienceAlert
  3. Mystery of Tyrannosaurus rex’s Tiny Arms May Finally Have an Answer  Sci.News
  4. Why meat-eating dinosaurs like T. rex evolved tiny arms  Phys.org
  5. Why were T. rex’s arms so tiny? Paleontologists finally find an answer.  Popular Science

item.title: The human genome contains traces of ancient viruses that infected our ancestors millions of years ago — and some of those viral leftovers were later repurposed into genes that help make human pregnancy possible - Space Daily
item.description: The human genome contains traces of ancient viruses that infected our ancestors millions of years ago — and some of those viral leftovers were later repurposed into genes that help make human pregnancy possible  Space Daily

item.title: An ancient solar storm left clues in tree rings and a famous poet's diary: 'Red lights in the northern sky' - Space
item.description:
1. An ancient solar storm left clues in tree rings and a famous poet's diary: 'Red lights in the northern sky'  Space
  2. Deadly “red sky” solar storm from 800 years ago discovered in ancient trees  ScienceDaily
  3. A red sky frightened medieval Japan, and buried trees now reveal the solar storm behind it  Earth.com

item.title: The Apollo astronauts left mirrors on the Moon that scientists still bounce lasers off 57 years later, and the round-trip measurement is precise enough to track the Moon drifting away from Earth at the speed your fingernails grow - Space Daily
item.description: The Apollo astronauts left mirrors on the Moon that scientists still bounce lasers off 57 years later, and the round-trip measurement is precise enough to track the Moon drifting away from Earth at the speed your fingernails grow  Space Daily

item.title: NASA deliberately crashed Galileo into Jupiter in 2003 to protect Europa, after the spacecraft found signs of the ocean it could one day contaminate - Space Daily
item.description: NASA deliberately crashed Galileo into Jupiter in 2003 to protect Europa, after the spacecraft found signs of the ocean it could one day contaminate  Space Daily

item.title: The Cassini spacecraft was deliberately flown into Saturn in 2017 because NASA refused to risk contaminating Enceladus, and in its final 90 seconds its thrusters fought the atmosphere so it could keep sending data home - Space Daily
item.description: The Cassini spacecraft was deliberately flown into Saturn in 2017 because NASA refused to risk contaminating Enceladus, and in its final 90 seconds its thrusters fought the atmosphere so it could keep sending data home  Space Daily

item.title: The Huygens probe descended by parachute to the surface of Titan in 2005, through an orange haze colder than minus 170 degrees Celsius, and more than a billion kilometres from home it remains the only spacecraft humanity has ever landed anywhere in t - Space Daily

item.description:

  3. 146,000-Year-Old Discovery Rewrites the Story of Human Creativity  SciTechDaily
  4. Ice Age discovery in China challenges theories about origins of human creativity  Mid-day

item.title: Researchers simulated 30 million routes to the Moon and found a hidden detour through L1 that saves fuel and keeps spacecraft talking to Earth the whole way - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Researchers simulated 30 million routes to the Moon and found a hidden detour through L1 that saves fuel and keeps spacecraft talking to Earth the whole way  Space Daily
  2. Scientists discover a shortcut to the moon that NASA missed  Yahoo
  3. Scientists find a hidden route to the moon that saves fuel  Space
  4. Gravity Highways: "Hidden Space Roads" Break the Cost Barrier for Moon Missions  صوت الإمارات
  5. Scientists Map Most Cost-Effective Route to the Moon Out of 24 Million Possibilities  ZME Science

item.description: Physicists figure out how to reduce formation of 'viscous fingers'  Phys.org
